title: Fitchers Vogel
source: Brüder Grimm, Kinder- und Hausmärchen, Band 1, 7. Auflage (Ausgabe letzter Hand), Dieterich, Göttingen 1857, KHM 46. Text from Wikisource (twice proofread against the original). 1857 spelling preserved exactly.
about: Fitcher’s Bird (KHM 46) is the Grimms’ German Bluebeard: a sorcerer carries off three sisters in turn, and a forbidden bloody chamber and a tell-tale egg betray the first two, who are murdered and hacked to pieces. The clever third sister sets the egg safely aside, revives and reassembles her sisters, smuggles them home hidden in the sorcerer’s own basket, disguises herself in honey and feathers as the strange “Fitcher’s bird,” and brings her kinsmen to burn the sorcerer and his crew. Spelling follows the 1857 edition, so you will meet older forms such as gieng, fieng, erschrack, thun, Thüre, Athem, Todtenkopf, todte and rothen.

sentences.t: Es war einmal ein Hexenmeister, der nahm die Gestalt eines armen Mannes an, gieng vor die Häuser und bettelte, und fieng die schönen Mädchen.
sentences.l: There was once a sorcerer, who took on the shape of a poor man, went before the houses and begged, and caught the beautiful girls.
sentences.i: Once upon a time there was a sorcerer who took on the shape of a poor man, went from house to house begging, and carried off the beautiful girls.
sentences.n: The classic fairy-tale opening, “Es war einmal” = ‘once upon a time.’ A Hexenmeister is a male witch, a sorcerer (Hexe ‘witch’ + Meister). “nahm … an” is the separable verb annehmen (‘to take on, assume’); the prefix an- jumps to the end — you will meet this constantly. “gieng” and “fieng” are the 1857 spellings of ging and fing (gehen, fangen).

sentences.t: Kein Mensch wußte wo er sie hinbrachte, denn sie kamen nie wieder zum Vorschein.
sentences.l: No person knew where he took them, for they never came again to appearance.
sentences.i: No one knew where he took them off to, for they never came to light again.
sentences.n: “zum Vorschein kommen” is a set phrase, ‘to come into view, come to light.’ “hinbrachte” (hinbringen) — the prefix hin- marks motion away to a place. “wußte” is the 1857 spelling of wusste.

sentences.t: Eines Tages erschien er vor der Thüre eines Mannes, der drei schöne Töchter hatte, sah aus wie ein armer schwacher Bettler und trug eine Kötze auf dem Rücken, als wollte er milde Gaben darin sammeln.
sentences.l: One day he appeared before the door of a man who had three beautiful daughters, looked like a poor weak beggar and carried a pannier on his back, as if he wanted to gather alms in it.
sentences.i: One day he appeared at the door of a man who had three beautiful daughters; he looked like a poor, feeble beggar and carried a pannier on his back, as though he meant to collect alms in it.
sentences.n: “Thüre” carries the old -th- (modern Tür). A Kötze is a large basket carried on the back, a pannier — it will matter later. “milde Gaben” = alms, charitable gifts. “als wollte er” = ‘as if he wanted’ — als + subjunctive drops the daß.

sentences.t: Er bat um ein bischen Essen, und als die älteste herauskam und ihm ein Stück Brot reichen wollte, rührte er sie nur an, und sie mußte in seine Kötze springen.
sentences.l: He asked for a little bit of food, and when the eldest came out and wanted to hand him a piece of bread, he only touched her, and she had to spring into his pannier.
sentences.i: He begged for a bit of food, and when the eldest came out and was about to hand him a piece of bread, he merely touched her, and she was forced to leap into his pannier.
sentences.n: “bischen” is the 1857 spelling of bißchen/bisschen (‘a little bit’). “rührte … an” is the separable verb anrühren (‘to touch’). The touch is his magic: she cannot resist, and “mußte … springen” (‘had to leap’) shows the compulsion.

sentences.t: Darauf eilte er mit starken Schritten fort und trug sie in einen finstern Wald zu seinem Haus, das mitten darin stand.
sentences.l: Thereupon he hurried away with strong strides and carried her into a dark forest to his house, which stood in the middle of it.
sentences.i: Then he hurried off with great strides and carried her into a dark forest, to his house that stood in the midst of it.
sentences.n: “eilte … fort” is the separable verb forteilen (‘to hurry away’). “finstern” is an old inflected form of finster (‘dark, gloomy’). “mitten darin” = right in the middle of it.

sentences.t: In dem Haus war alles prächtig: er gab ihr was sie nur wünschte und sprach „mein Schatz, es wird dir wohlgefallen bei mir, du hast alles was dein Herz begehrt.“
sentences.l: In the house everything was splendid: he gave her whatever she only wished and spoke, “my treasure, it will please you well with me, you have everything that your heart desires.”
sentences.i: In the house everything was magnificent; he gave her whatever she wished and said, “My darling, you will be well content with me — you have everything your heart desires.”
sentences.n: “Schatz” (‘treasure’) is a term of endearment, ‘darling.’ “wohlgefallen” = to please well (wohl + gefallen), taking the dative dir. “begehrt” (begehren) = to crave, desire.

sentences.t: Das dauerte ein paar Tage, da sagte er „ich muß fortreisen und dich eine kurze Zeit allein lassen, da sind die Hausschlüssel, du kannst überall hingehen und alles betrachten, nur nicht in eine Stube, die dieser kleine Schlüssel da aufschließt, das verbiet ich dir bei Lebensstrafe.“
sentences.l: That lasted a couple of days, then he said, “I must travel away and leave you alone a short time; there are the house-keys, you can go everywhere and look at everything, only not into one room that this little key here unlocks — that I forbid you on pain of death.”
sentences.i: That went on for a few days; then he said, “I must go away and leave you alone for a short while. Here are the house-keys; you may go everywhere and look at everything — only not into one room that this little key here unlocks; that I forbid you on pain of death.”
sentences.n: This is the Bluebeard prohibition — the forbidden room and the one small key. “fortreisen” (separable) = to travel away. “aufschließt” (aufschließen) = to unlock. “bei Lebensstrafe” = on pain of death (Lebensstrafe, the death penalty). “verbiet” = verbiete, ‘I forbid.’

sentences.t: Auch gab er ihr ein Ei und sprach „das Ei verwahre mir sorgfältig und trag es lieber beständig bei dir, denn gienge es verloren, so würde ein großes Unglück daraus entstehen.“
sentences.l: He also gave her an egg and spoke, “keep the egg carefully for me and rather carry it constantly with you, for were it to be lost, then a great misfortune would arise from it.”
sentences.i: He also gave her an egg and said, “Guard this egg carefully for me, and you had best carry it about with you always; for if it were lost, a great misfortune would come of it.”
sentences.n: The tell-tale egg: like the key, it is a magical test the sorcerer sets. “verwahre” and “trag” are imperatives (verwahren ‘to keep safe,’ tragen). “gienge es verloren, so würde …” is a subjunctive II conditional (‘were it lost, then would …’) with the ‘if’ expressed by putting the verb first. “beständig” = constantly. “entstehen” = to arise, come about.

sentences.t: Sie nahm die Schlüssel und das Ei, und versprach alles wohl auszurichten.
sentences.l: She took the keys and the egg, and promised to carry everything out well.
sentences.i: She took the keys and the egg and promised to see to everything properly.
sentences.n: “auszurichten” (ausrichten) here = to carry out, accomplish (a task); the zu-infinitive of a separable verb splits as aus-zu-richten.

sentences.t: Als er fort war, gieng sie in dem Haus herum von unten bis oben und besah alles, die Stuben glänzten von Silber und Gold, und sie meinte sie hätte nie so große Pracht gesehen.
sentences.l: When he was away, she went around in the house from bottom to top and looked at everything; the rooms gleamed with silver and gold, and she thought she had never seen such great splendor.
sentences.i: When he was gone, she went about the house from bottom to top and looked at everything; the rooms shone with silver and gold, and she thought she had never seen such great splendour.
sentences.n: “gieng … herum” (herumgehen) = to go about. “von unten bis oben” (‘from bottom to top’) recurs — it comes back in the bird’s riddle-verse. “sie hätte … gesehen” is subjunctive II of reported thought (‘she thought she had never seen’).

sentences.t: Endlich kam sie auch zu der verbotenen Thür, sie wollte vorüber gehen, aber die Neugierde ließ ihr keine Ruhe.
sentences.l: At last she came also to the forbidden door; she wanted to go past, but the curiosity let her no peace.
sentences.i: At last she came to the forbidden door too; she meant to pass it by, but curiosity gave her no rest.
sentences.n: “die Neugierde ließ ihr keine Ruhe” = ‘curiosity gave her no peace’ (jemandem keine Ruhe lassen). “verbotenen” is the past participle of verbieten used as an adjective, ‘forbidden.’ “vorüber gehen” (vorübergehen) = to go past.

sentences.t: Sie besah den Schlüssel, er sah aus wie ein anderer, sie steckte ihn ein und drehte ein wenig, da sprang die Thüre auf.
sentences.l: She looked at the key; it looked like any other; she put it in and turned a little, then the door sprang open.
sentences.i: She examined the key; it looked like any other; she put it in and turned it a little, and the door sprang open.
sentences.n: “sah aus” (aussehen, ‘to look, appear’), “steckte … ein” (einstecken, ‘to put in’) and “sprang … auf” (aufspringen, ‘to spring open’) are all separable verbs — three in one sentence.

sentences.t: Aber was erblickte sie als sie hineintrat?
sentences.l: But what did she catch sight of when she stepped in?
sentences.i: But what did she behold when she stepped inside?
sentences.n: “erblicken” = to catch sight of, behold. “hineintrat” (hineintreten) = to step in — a rhetorical question bracing the reader for the horror to come.

sentences.t: ein großes blutiges Becken stand in der Mitte, und darin lagen todte zerhauene Menschen, daneben stand ein Holzblock und ein blinkendes Beil lag darauf.
sentences.l: a great bloody basin stood in the middle, and in it lay dead hacked-apart people; beside it stood a wooden block, and a gleaming axe lay upon it.
sentences.i: a great bloody basin stood in the middle, and in it lay dead, dismembered people; beside it stood a chopping-block, with a gleaming axe lying on it.
sentences.n: The bloody chamber, at the heart of every Bluebeard tale. “zerhauene” (from zerhauen) = hacked apart; the prefix zer- means ‘to pieces.’ “todte” is the 1857 spelling of tote (‘dead’), with the old -dt. “Beil” = axe, hatchet; “Holzblock” = the wooden chopping-block.

sentences.t: Sie erschrack so sehr, daß das Ei, das sie in der Hand hielt, hineinplumpte.
sentences.l: She took fright so greatly that the egg, which she held in her hand, plopped in.
sentences.i: She was so terrified that the egg she held in her hand plopped in.
sentences.n: The fatal moment: the egg falls into the blood. “erschrack” is the 1857/archaic preterite of erschrecken (‘took fright’; modern erschrak). “hineinplumpte” (hineinplumpen) is expressive — plumpen imitates the ‘plop’ of something falling in.

sentences.t: Sie holte es wieder heraus und wischte das Blut ab, aber vergeblich, es kam den Augenblick wieder zum Vorschein; sie wischte und schabte, aber sie konnte es nicht herunter kriegen.
sentences.l: She fetched it out again and wiped the blood off, but in vain — it came to view again the same moment; she wiped and scraped, but she could not get it off.
sentences.i: She fetched it out again and wiped off the blood, but in vain — it reappeared the very same moment; she wiped and scraped, but she could not get it off.
sentences.n: This is the tell-tale stain: the egg is enchanted, so the blood cannot be wiped away and will betray her. “holte … heraus” (herausholen) and “wischte … ab” (abwischen) are separable. “kriegen” is colloquial for bekommen (‘to get’).

sentences.t: Nicht lange, so kam der Mann von der Reise zurück, und das erste was er forderte war der Schlüssel und das Ei.
sentences.l: Not long, then the man came back from the journey, and the first thing he demanded was the key and the egg.
sentences.i: Before long the man came back from his journey, and the first thing he demanded was the key and the egg.
sentences.n: “Nicht lange, so …” — an elliptical ‘it was not long before …’ “kam … zurück” (zurückkommen) is separable. “forderte” (fordern) = to demand.

sentences.t: Sie reichte es ihm hin, aber sie zitterte dabei, und er sah gleich an den rothen Flecken daß sie in der Blutkammer gewesen war.
sentences.l: She handed it to him, but she trembled at it, and he saw at once by the red spots that she had been in the blood-chamber.
sentences.i: She held it out to him, but she trembled as she did so, and he saw at once by the red stains that she had been in the chamber of blood.
sentences.n: “reichte … hin” (hinreichen) = to hand over. “rothen” is the 1857 spelling of roten (‘red’), with old -th-. “an den rothen Flecken … sah” — sehen/erkennen an + dative = to tell by something. Blutkammer = the chamber of blood.

sentences.t: „Bist du gegen meinen Willen in die Kammer gegangen,“ sprach er, „so sollst du gegen deinen Willen wieder hinein. Dein Leben ist zu Ende.“
sentences.l: “Have you gone against my will into the chamber,” he spoke, “then you shall against your will go in again. Your life is at an end.”
sentences.i: “If you have gone into the chamber against my will,” he said, “then against your will you shall go in again. Your life is at an end.”
sentences.n: A conditional formed by fronting the verb: “Bist du … gegangen, so …” = ‘if you have gone …, then …’ Note the grim symmetry, gegen meinen Willen / gegen deinen Willen (‘against my will / against your will’). The verb of motion is dropped after sollst: “du sollst … wieder hinein [gehen].”

sentences.t: Er warf sie nieder, schleifte sie an den Haaren hin, schlug ihr das Haupt auf dem Blocke ab und zerhackte sie, daß ihr Blut auf dem Boden dahin floß.
sentences.l: He threw her down, dragged her by the hair along, struck her head off on the block and hacked her to pieces, so that her blood flowed away over the floor.
sentences.i: He threw her down, dragged her along by the hair, struck off her head on the block, and hacked her to pieces, so that her blood ran out over the floor.
sentences.n: Grimm does not soften the murder. Three separable verbs: “warf … nieder” (niederwerfen), “schleifte … hin” (hinschleifen, ‘to drag along’), “schlug … ab” (abschlagen, ‘to strike off’). “das Haupt” is an elevated word for ‘head.’ “zerhackte” (zerhacken) = to hack to pieces. “Blocke” has the old dative -e.

sentences.t: Dann warf er sie zu den übrigen ins Becken.
sentences.l: Then he threw her to the others into the basin.
sentences.i: Then he threw her in with the rest, into the basin.
sentences.n: “die übrigen” = the rest, the remaining ones. “ins” = in das.

sentences.t: „Jetzt will ich mir die zweite holen“ sprach der Hexenmeister, gieng wieder in Gestalt eines armen Mannes vor das Haus und bettelte.
sentences.l: “Now I will fetch myself the second one,” spoke the sorcerer, went again in the shape of a poor man before the house and begged.
sentences.i: “Now I will go and fetch the second one,” said the sorcerer; he went once more in the shape of a poor man to the house and begged.
sentences.n: “mir … holen” — the dative mir is a dative of interest (‘fetch for myself’). “in Gestalt eines armen Mannes” = in the shape of a poor man (Gestalt, ‘form, shape’).

sentences.t: Da brachte ihm die zweite ein Stück Brot, er fieng sie wie die erste durch bloßes Anrühren und trug sie fort.
sentences.l: Then the second brought him a piece of bread; he caught her like the first through mere touching and carried her away.
sentences.i: Then the second brought him a piece of bread; he caught her, like the first, by a mere touch, and carried her off.
sentences.n: “durch bloßes Anrühren” = by mere touching (bloß ‘mere,’ Anrühren the noun from anrühren). “trug … fort” (forttragen) is separable.

sentences.t: Es ergieng ihr nicht besser als ihrer Schwester, sie ließ sich von ihrer Neugierde verleiten, öffnete die Blutkammer und schaute hinein, und mußte es bei seiner Rückkehr mit dem Leben büßen.
sentences.l: It fared no better for her than for her sister; she let herself be led astray by her curiosity, opened the blood-chamber and looked in, and had to pay for it with her life at his return.
sentences.i: It went no better with her than with her sister; she let curiosity lead her astray, opened the chamber of blood and looked in, and had to pay for it with her life when he returned.
sentences.n: “es ergieng ihr” — the impersonal ergehen + dative, ‘it fared with her’ (ergieng is the 1857 -ie- spelling). “sich verleiten lassen” = to let oneself be led astray. “mit dem Leben büßen” = to pay with one’s life (büßen ‘to atone for’). “Rückkehr” = return.

sentences.t: Er gieng nun und holte die dritte, die aber war klug und listig.
sentences.l: He went now and fetched the third, but she was clever and cunning.
sentences.i: He went now and fetched the third; but she was clever and cunning.
sentences.n: “die aber war klug” — the fronted demonstrative die points back to ‘the third (daughter),’ and aber (‘but’) sits in second place. “listig” = crafty, cunning. This is the turn of the tale: the clever sister.

sentences.t: Als er ihr die Schlüssel und das Ei gegeben hatte und fortgereist war, verwahrte sie das Ei erst sorgfältig, dann besah sie das Haus und gieng zuletzt in die verbotene Kammer.
sentences.l: When he had given her the keys and the egg and had traveled away, she first put the egg carefully away, then looked over the house and went at last into the forbidden chamber.
sentences.i: When he had given her the keys and the egg and had gone away, she first put the egg carefully aside, then looked the house over, and went last of all into the forbidden chamber.
sentences.n: The clever sister’s decisive move: she sets the egg safely aside (“verwahrte … erst sorgfältig”) before exploring, so it can never fall into the blood. “fortgereist” is the past participle of fortreisen. “verbotene” = forbidden.

sentences.t: Ach, was erblickte sie!
sentences.l: Ah, what did she catch sight of!
sentences.i: Oh, what a sight she beheld!
sentences.n: A rhetorical exclamation (not a question here) — ‘what she saw!’ — echoing her sisters’ discovery.

sentences.t: ihre beiden lieben Schwestern lagen da in dem Becken jämmerlich ermordet und zerhackt.
sentences.l: her two dear sisters lay there in the basin, miserably murdered and hacked to pieces.
sentences.i: her two dear sisters lay there in the basin, piteously murdered and hacked to pieces.
sentences.n: “jämmerlich” = wretchedly, piteously. “ermordet” (ermorden) and “zerhackt” (zerhacken) are past participles, ‘murdered’ and ‘hacked to pieces.’

sentences.t: Aber sie hub an und suchte die Glieder zusammen und legte sie zurecht, Kopf, Leib, Arm und Beine.
sentences.l: But she began and sought the limbs together and laid them in order — head, body, arm and legs.
sentences.i: But she set to work and gathered the limbs together and laid them in place — head, body, arms and legs.
sentences.n: “hub an” is the archaic preterite of anheben (‘to begin, set to’); modern German has hob an. “suchte … zusammen” (zusammensuchen) = to gather up; “legte … zurecht” (zurechtlegen) = to lay out in order. Both separable. Glieder = limbs.

sentences.t: Und als nichts mehr fehlte, da fiengen die Glieder an sich zu regen und schlossen sich an einander, und beide Mädchen öffneten die Augen und waren wieder lebendig.
sentences.l: And when nothing was missing any more, then the limbs began to stir and joined themselves to one another, and both girls opened their eyes and were alive again.
sentences.i: And when nothing was missing any more, the limbs began to stir and knit themselves together, and both girls opened their eyes and were alive again.
sentences.n: “fiengen … an” is the 1857 spelling of fingen an (anfangen, ‘to begin’), separable. “sich regen” = to stir, move. “schlossen sich an einander” — schließen here of the limbs closing/joining together. The resurrection is the tale’s wonder — undoing the dismemberment.

sentences.t: Da freuten sie sich, küßten und herzten einander.
sentences.l: Then they rejoiced, kissed and embraced one another.
sentences.i: Then they rejoiced, and kissed and embraced one another.
sentences.n: “sich freuen” = to rejoice, be glad (reflexive). “herzen” = to hug, embrace (from Herz, ‘heart’). einander = one another.

sentences.t: Der Mann forderte bei seiner Ankunft gleich Schlüssel und Ei, und als er keine Spur von Blut daran entdecken konnte, sprach er „du hast die Probe bestanden, du sollst meine Braut sein.“
sentences.l: The man demanded on his arrival at once key and egg, and when he could discover no trace of blood on it, he spoke, “you have passed the test, you shall be my bride.”
sentences.i: On his arrival the man at once demanded the key and the egg, and when he could find no trace of blood on it, he said, “You have passed the test; you shall be my bride.”
sentences.n: “keine Spur von Blut” = no trace of blood (Spur, ‘track, trace’). “die Probe bestehen” = to pass the test (bestanden, past participle of bestehen). “entdecken” = to discover, detect. Because the egg stayed clean, the sorcerer’s power over her is broken.

sentences.t: Er hatte jetzt keine Macht mehr über sie und mußte thun was sie verlangte.
sentences.l: He had now no power any more over her and had to do what she demanded.
sentences.i: He now had no more power over her and had to do whatever she asked.
sentences.n: “keine Macht mehr über sie” = no more power over her — the reversal is complete. “thun” is the 1857 spelling of tun (‘to do’), with the old th-. “verlangte” (verlangen) = to demand, ask.

sentences.t: „Wohlan,“ antwortete sie, „du sollst vorher einen Korb voll Gold meinem Vater und meiner Mutter bringen und es selbst auf deinem Rücken hintragen; derweil will ich die Hochzeit bestellen.“
sentences.l: “Well then,” she answered, “you shall first bring a basket full of gold to my father and my mother and carry it yourself on your back; meanwhile I will arrange the wedding.”
sentences.i: “Very well,” she answered, “first you shall bring a basket full of gold to my father and mother, and carry it yourself on your back; meanwhile I will make ready the wedding.”
sentences.n: “Wohlan” is an old interjection, ‘well then, come now.’ Her scheme begins: the basket (Korb) that will carry her sisters home. “hintragen” = to carry there (separable hin-). “derweil” = meanwhile. “die Hochzeit bestellen” = to arrange/order the wedding.

sentences.t: Dann lief sie zu ihren Schwestern, die sie in einem Kämmerlein versteckt hatte und sagte „der Augenblick ist da, wo ich euch retten kann: der Bösewicht soll euch selbst wieder heimtragen; aber sobald ihr zu Hause seid, sendet mir Hilfe.“
sentences.l: Then she ran to her sisters, whom she had hidden in a little chamber, and said, “the moment is here in which I can save you: the villain himself shall carry you home again; but as soon as you are at home, send me help.”
sentences.i: Then she ran to her sisters, whom she had hidden in a little chamber, and said, “The moment has come when I can save you: the villain himself shall carry you home again; but as soon as you are home, send me help.”
sentences.n: Kämmerlein is a diminutive of Kammer (‘little chamber’) — Grimm’s -lein diminutives are everywhere. “versteckt hatte” (verstecken) = had hidden. “heimtragen” = to carry home (separable heim-). “sobald” = as soon as; “sendet” is the plural imperative of senden (‘send!’).

sentences.t: Sie setzte beide in einen Korb und deckte sie mit Gold ganz zu, daß nichts von ihnen zu sehen war, dann rief sie den Hexenmeister herein und sprach „nun trag den Korb fort, aber daß du mir unterwegs nicht stehen bleibst und ruhest, ich schaue durch mein Fensterlein und habe acht.“
sentences.l: She set both into a basket and covered them over entirely with gold, so that nothing of them was to be seen; then she called the sorcerer in and spoke, “now carry the basket away, but see that on the way you do not stand still and rest — I look through my little window and keep watch.”
sentences.i: She set them both into a basket and covered them completely with gold, so that nothing of them could be seen; then she called the sorcerer in and said, “Now carry the basket off; but mind that you do not stop and rest on the way — I am looking through my little window and keeping watch.”
sentences.n: “deckte … zu” (zudecken) = to cover up (separable). “daß du … nicht stehen bleibst und ruhest” — a daß-clause of warning; ruhest keeps the old -est ending (modern ruhst). Fensterlein is a diminutive of Fenster (‘little window’); “acht haben” = to keep watch, pay heed. Her trick: he will think she can see him the whole way.

sentences.t: Der Hexenmeister hob den Korb auf seinen Rücken und gieng damit fort, er drückte ihn aber so schwer, daß ihm der Schweiß über das Angesicht lief.
sentences.l: The sorcerer heaved the basket onto his back and went away with it, but it pressed on him so heavily that the sweat ran over his face.
sentences.i: The sorcerer heaved the basket onto his back and set off with it; but it bore down so heavily that the sweat ran down his face.
sentences.n: “hob … auf” (aufheben, ‘to lift up’) and “gieng … fort” (fortgehen) are separable. “ihm … lief” — the dative ihm (‘ran down his face’), a dative of possession German prefers to a possessive. Angesicht = face (elevated). Schweiß = sweat.

sentences.t: Da setzte er sich nieder und wollte ein wenig ruhen, aber gleich rief eine im Korbe „ich schaue durch mein Fensterlein und sehe daß du ruhst, willst du gleich weiter.“
sentences.l: Then he sat himself down and wanted to rest a little, but at once one in the basket called, “I look through my little window and see that you are resting — will you get on at once.”
sentences.i: Then he sat down and wanted to rest a little; but at once one of them in the basket called, “I am looking through my little window and I see that you are resting — get on with you at once!”
sentences.n: “setzte sich nieder” (sich niedersetzen) = to sit down (separable reflexive). “eine im Korbe” = ‘one (of the sisters) in the basket.’ “willst du gleich weiter” — an impatient command with the verb of motion dropped, ‘will you get on at once.’ The sisters, hidden, do the ‘watching’ for her.

sentences.t: Er meinte die Braut rief ihm das zu und machte sich wieder auf.
sentences.l: He thought the bride called that to him and set himself off again.
sentences.i: He thought it was the bride calling it to him, and set off again.
sentences.n: “rief … zu” (zurufen) = to call out to (separable). “machte sich … auf” (sich aufmachen) = to set out (separable reflexive). He is fooled — he believes the voice is the bride watching from her window.

sentences.t: Nochmals wollte er sich setzen, aber es rief gleich „ich schaue durch mein Fensterlein und sehe daß du ruhst, willst du gleich weiter.“
sentences.l: Once more he wanted to sit down, but it called at once, “I look through my little window and see that you are resting — will you get on at once.”
sentences.i: Once more he wanted to sit down, but at once it called, “I am looking through my little window and I see that you are resting — get on with you at once!”
sentences.n: “Nochmals” = once again. The refrain repeats, folktale-fashion, each time he tries to rest — the same phrase word for word.

sentences.t: Und so oft er stillstand, rief es, und da mußte er fort, bis er endlich stöhnend und außer Athem den Korb mit dem Gold und den beiden Mädchen in ihrer Eltern Haus brachte.
sentences.l: And as often as he stood still, it called, and then he had to go on, until at last, groaning and out of breath, he brought the basket with the gold and the two girls into their parents’ house.
sentences.i: And every time he stood still, it called out, and then he had to go on, until at last, groaning and out of breath, he brought the basket — with the gold and the two girls — to their parents’ house.
sentences.n: “so oft” = as often as, every time. “stillstand” (stillstehen) = to stand still (separable). “außer Athem” = out of breath; Athem is the 1857 spelling of Atem, with the old -th-. “in ihrer Eltern Haus” — ihrer Eltern is genitive plural, ‘their parents’.’

sentences.t: Daheim aber ordnete die Braut das Hochzeitfest an und ließ die Freunde des Hexenmeisters dazu einladen.
sentences.l: At home, however, the bride arranged the wedding feast and had the sorcerer’s friends invited to it.
sentences.i: At home, meanwhile, the bride arranged the wedding feast and had the sorcerer’s friends invited to it.
sentences.n: “ordnete … an” (anordnen) = to arrange, order (separable). “ließ … einladen” — lassen + infinitive, ‘had (them) invited’ (causative). Hochzeitfest = wedding feast.

sentences.t: Dann nahm sie einen Todtenkopf mit grinsenden Zähnen, setzte ihm einen Schmuck auf und einen Blumenkranz, trug ihn oben vors Bodenloch und ließ ihn da hinausschauen.
sentences.l: Then she took a skull with grinning teeth, set an ornament on it and a garland of flowers, carried it up before the loft-hatch and let it look out there.
sentences.i: Then she took a skull with grinning teeth, set an ornament and a garland of flowers upon it, carried it up to the loft-hatch, and let it look out from there.
sentences.n: Her disguise-trick begins. Todtenkopf (1857 spelling; modern Totenkopf) = skull, death’s-head; “grinsenden Zähnen” = grinning teeth. “setzte … auf” (aufsetzen) = to put on. Blumenkranz = garland of flowers. “vors Bodenloch” = vor das Bodenloch, before the loft-hatch (Bodenloch, the opening to the attic/loft). “hinausschauen” = to look out (separable).

sentences.t: Als alles bereit war, steckte sie sich in ein Faß mit Honig, schnitt das Bett auf und wälzte sich darin, daß sie aussah wie ein wunderlicher Vogel und kein Mensch sie erkennen konnte.
sentences.l: When everything was ready, she stuck herself into a barrel of honey, cut the bed open and rolled herself in it, so that she looked like a strange bird and no person could recognize her.
sentences.i: When all was ready, she dipped herself into a barrel of honey, cut open the featherbed and rolled about in it, until she looked like some strange bird, and no one could recognize her.
sentences.n: The feathered-bird disguise: honey to make the feathers stick, the slit featherbed for the down. This is how she becomes “Fitchers Vogel.” “steckte sich … in” = put herself into; Faß (modern Fass) = barrel. “schnitt … auf” (aufschneiden) = to cut open. “wälzte sich” = rolled herself. “wunderlicher Vogel” = strange, curious bird.

sentences.t: Da gieng sie zum Haus hinaus, und unterwegs begegnete ihr ein Theil der Hochzeitsgäste, die fragten
sentences.l: Then she went out of the house, and on the way a part of the wedding guests met her, who asked
sentences.i: Then she went out of the house, and on the way she was met by some of the wedding guests, who asked:
sentences.n: “gieng … hinaus” (hinausgehen) = to go out (separable). “begegnete ihr” — begegnen takes the dative (‘met her’). Theil is the 1857 spelling of Teil (‘part’), with the old -th-. Hochzeitsgäste = wedding guests. The sentence runs straight into the verse that follows.

sentences.t:
„Du Fitchers Vogel, wo kommst du her?“
„Ich komme von Fitze Fitchers Hause her.“
„Was macht denn da die junge Braut?“
„Hat gekehrt von unten bis oben das Haus,
und guckt zum Bodenloch heraus.“
sentences.l:
“You Fitcher’s bird, where do you come from?”
“I come from Fitze Fitcher’s house.”
“What is the young bride doing there, then?”
“She has swept the house from bottom to top,
and peeps out of the loft-hatch.”
sentences.i:
“You, Fitcher’s bird, where do you come from?”
“I come from Fitze Fitcher’s house.”
“And what is the young bride doing there?”
“She has swept the house from bottom to top,
and peeps out from the loft-hatch.”
sentences.n: The riddle-verse that gives the tale its name. “Fitchers Vogel” = ‘Fitcher’s bird’; Fitcher (and the jingling Fitze Fitcher) is the sorcerer’s name — its meaning is obscure, kept for its sound. The disguised bride answers her own questioners, describing the decked-out skull at the loft-hatch as though it were herself. “gekehrt” is the past participle of kehren (‘to sweep’); “guckt” (gucken) = peeps, peers. “von unten bis oben” returns from earlier.

sentences.t: Endlich begegnete ihr der Bräutigam, der langsam zurück wanderte.
sentences.l: At last the bridegroom met her, who was slowly wandering back.
sentences.i: At last the bridegroom met her, making his way slowly back.
sentences.n: “begegnete ihr” — again begegnen + dative. “langsam zurück wanderte” — the sorcerer trudges home after his exhausting errand; Bräutigam = bridegroom.

sentences.t: Er fragte wie die andern
sentences.l: He asked like the others
sentences.i: He asked, like the others:
sentences.n: The line breaks off into the repeated verse — the bridegroom asks the very same riddle the guests asked.

sentences.t:
„Du Fitchers Vogel, wo kommst du her?“
„Ich komme von Fitze Fitchers Hause her.“
„Was macht denn da meine junge Braut?“
„Hat gekehrt von unten bis oben das Haus,
und guckt zum Bodenloch heraus.“
sentences.l:
“You Fitcher’s bird, where do you come from?”
“I come from Fitze Fitcher’s house.”
“What is my young bride doing there, then?”
“She has swept the house from bottom to top,
and peeps out of the loft-hatch.”
sentences.i:
“You, Fitcher’s bird, where do you come from?”
“I come from Fitze Fitcher’s house.”
“And what is my young bride doing there?”
“She has swept the house from bottom to top,
and peeps out from the loft-hatch.”
sentences.n: The verse repeats almost exactly, with one telling change: the bridegroom says “meine junge Braut” (‘my young bride’) where the guests said “die junge Braut.’ He is answered as if she were safely at home — the skull at the hatch playing her part.

sentences.t: Der Bräutigam schaute hinauf und sah den geputzten Todtenkopf, da meinte er es wäre seine Braut und nickte ihr zu und grüßte sie freundlich.
sentences.l: The bridegroom looked up and saw the decked-out skull; then he thought it was his bride and nodded to her and greeted her in a friendly way.
sentences.i: The bridegroom looked up and saw the decked-out skull, and thought it was his bride, and nodded to her and greeted her kindly.
sentences.n: “schaute hinauf” (hinaufschauen) = to look up. “geputzten” (from putzen) = adorned, decked out. “es wäre seine Braut” — subjunctive II of his mistaken belief (‘he thought it was his bride’). “nickte ihr zu” (zunicken) = to nod to (separable, + dative).

sentences.t: Wie er aber sammt seinen Gästen ins Haus gegangen war, da langten die Brüder und Verwandte der Braut an, die zu ihrer Rettung gesendet waren.
sentences.l: But when he, together with his guests, had gone into the house, then the brothers and relatives of the bride arrived, who had been sent to her rescue.
sentences.i: But when he, together with his guests, had gone into the house, the bride’s brothers and kinsmen arrived, who had been sent to her rescue.
sentences.n: “sammt” (modern samt) = together with (+ dative). “langten … an” (anlangen, ‘to arrive’) is separable. “gesendet” = sent (senden). “zu ihrer Rettung” = to her rescue (Rettung) — the help she asked her sisters to send.

sentences.t: Sie schlossen alle Thüren des Hauses zu, daß niemand entfliehen konnte, und steckten es an, also daß der Hexenmeister mit sammt seinem Gesindel verbrennen mußte.
sentences.l: They locked all the doors of the house, so that no one could escape, and set it on fire, so that the sorcerer, together with all his rabble, had to burn.
sentences.i: They locked all the doors of the house, so that no one could escape, and set it ablaze, so that the sorcerer, together with all his crew, was burned to death.
sentences.n: The fiery ending. “schlossen … zu” (zuschließen, ‘to lock’) and “steckten … an” (anstecken, ‘to set on fire’) are separable. Thüren = doors (1857 th-); Hauses is genitive. “entfliehen” = to escape, flee. “mit sammt” intensifies samt, ‘along with the whole of.’ Gesindel = rabble, riffraff. “also daß” = ‘so that’ (result).
